explanation.text: Although William cannot attend school, he experiments with radios and a bicycle dynamo, visits the library, studies science diagrams, and looks up unfamiliar English. This shows that he continues learning independently rather than depending on formal classes.

William spends time with the books' drawings and diagrams and uses a dictionary or librarian to understand difficult words. This careful study shows that "pore over" means to examine something closely.

William searches discarded machinery for usable parts and transforms a tractor fan, plastic pipe, bamboo, bearings, and a piston into windmill components. These inventive substitutions show that he is resourceful despite having little money or equipment.

William ignores children who call him crazy and stays focused on building his machine. When the windmill lights the bulb, the same community cheers, which shows that his success convinces the people who had doubted him.

William's family gathers around the first electric light in their home after watching him build the windmill from scraps. Their reaction is one of wonder, so "marvel" suggests they are amazed by what he accomplished.

After William's windmill succeeds, news of the self-taught boy and his "electric wind" spreads on radio, in newspapers, and across the Internet. This shows an effect of success because his working invention brings him broad public recognition.

William's curiosity drives him to experiment and study science, while his persistence keeps him gathering parts and building despite poverty and ridicule. This shows that curiosity and persistence can create meaningful change for a family and community.

The opening identifies the two competing architects, the school-board meeting, their covered models, and the presentation-and-vote process. These details establish the setting and explain how the new school design will be selected.
Mr. Kowalski says that his entire concept has changed "markedly" and therefore needs new plans and extra presentation time. In this context, the word means that the change is clear and obvious.

Words such as "swooped," "graceful," "sunlit," and "modern" emphasize the model's dramatic beauty and innovation. This vivid description helps explain why the school board is astounded and applauds the design.

Before presenting, Mr. Kowalski repeatedly looks at Holling and nervously clears his throat. Once Holling realizes that Meryl Lee obtained his father's design from him, those uneasy looks support the conclusion that Kowalski knows he is using stolen plans.

Holling finally understands that Meryl Lee used their Valentine's Day meeting to obtain his father's school design. His statement that he "hadn't seen at all" what she was doing reveals that he did not know he was being deceived.

The first-person narration records Holling's observations of the architects, his father's anger, and Kowalski's repeated looks before Holling understands their meaning. This progression develops his point of view by moving from puzzled observation to a painful realization.

Mr. Kowalski and Mr. Hoodhood are presenting rival designs for the same new junior high school. Their competition belongs in a summary because it creates the story's main situation and explains the conflict over the copied plan.

Paragraph 3 explains why scientists once believed liquid water could not survive under Antarctica's extremely cold ice. The later paragraphs disprove that belief with flat-ice observations, radar data, satellite evidence, and the confirmed dimensions of Lake Vostok.

Paragraph 8 shifts from hidden Antarctic lakes to Galileo's evidence of an ocean beneath the icy surface of Europa. It therefore introduces the article's connection between research on Earth's extreme environments and the search for life in outer space.

Paragraph 10 asks how life without sunlight could obtain nutrients and withstand the pressure beneath miles of ice. These unanswered questions develop the central idea by identifying what scientists must determine about possible life in an extreme environment.

Ordinary drilling chemicals and surface microbes could pollute an isolated lake, so researchers at Lake Whillans used hot water, ultraviolet light, sterile equipment, and sterile clothing. This clean technology let them collect a water sample while addressing the contamination concern.
The chemicals and surface microbes could change the lake's water and make a sample no longer pure or reliable. In that context, "contaminate" means to pollute the isolated environment.

Radar and satellite evidence first confirmed liquid water beneath Antarctica's ice. Scientists then drilled with clean technology, tested a water sample, and found thousands of thriving microbes, which is the sequence by which the hidden life was discovered.

The author calls Antarctica's hidden wetland one of Earth's most valuable environments because it may guide the search for life elsewhere in the solar system. That evaluative claim reveals the view that Antarctica is exceptionally important to science.
